response.description_long:
Ledgerline sells relief from a specific monthly ritual: payment reconciliation for finance teams that sell across several processors. The site describes the status quo bluntly — exports from each processor, VLOOKUPs, manual matching of fees, refunds, and chargebacks, with teams spending the first week of every month closing the previous one.

The product works at the payout level. Read-only integrations pull transactions from Stripe, Adyen, PayPal, and bank feeds; for every deposit that hits the bank, the matching engine reconstructs the charges, refunds, chargebacks, and fees inside it, nets out currency conversion using the processor's own rate, and posts journal entries to NetSuite, QuickBooks, or Xero. Every match carries a confidence score. High-confidence matches post automatically under approved rules; everything else lands in a review queue with the evidence attached, and accepting a suggested resolution can turn it into a rule that resolves the same pattern next month. The site says exception queues typically shrink by more than half within three closes.

Trust posture is stated prominently: Ledgerline is SOC 2 Type II certified, every integration is read-only on the money side, and the platform never holds funds — it reads statements and reports but cannot initiate a payment. An audit trail records which rule matched, who approved it, and when, and auditors get a read-only seat with evidence exports.

The traction claims are aggregate: finance teams at over 400 companies use Ledgerline, customers report closing the books in hours instead of days, and month-end close times cut by 70 percent. Backfill covers up to 24 months of processor history, and bank feeds span over 10,000 institutions. Multi-entity support handles subsidiaries with separate ledgers.

The business model is usage-based — a flat platform fee plus a per-transaction rate that declines with volume, metered on transactions reconciled rather than ingested, with no implementation fee for standard integrations. The site contrasts itself against spreadsheets, generic RPA, and ERP reconciliation modules that match at the invoice level.

Ledgerline was founded in 2021 in New York by Elena Marsh, a former controller who ran month-end close at a multi-processor e-commerce company, and Daniel Okoye, a payments engineer who built payout systems inside a processor. The team is 32 people across New York and Toronto, and the founding observation still frames the product: both sides of reconciliation are generated by software, and only the matching in between was still manual.
